modify-and-verify-tracked-files.label: Modify and Verify Tracked Files
modify-and-verify-tracked-files.summary: Edit an existing tracked file, inspect the difference, stage the intended version, and verify the snapshot before committing.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.title: Spot a Modified Tracked File
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

`volunteer.md` is committed, but its meeting time changed. Use short status to identify the tracked modification.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: A tracked file remains known to Git after it changes; status marks the difference from HEAD.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

`volunteer.md` is committed, but its meeting time changed. Use short status to identify the tracked modification.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-notes
mkdir -p release-notes
git -C release-notes init -q -b main
git -C release-notes config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-notes
printf %s 'Meeting: 4 PM
' > volunteer.md
cd ..
git -C release-notes add -A
git -C release-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-notes
printf %s 'Meeting: 5 PM
' > volunteer.md
cd ..

git -C release-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-notes
printf %s 'Meeting: 4 PM
' > volunteer.md
cd ..
git -C release-notes add -A
git -C release-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-notes
printf %s 'Meeting: 5 PM
' > volunteer.md
cd ..

Run `git status --short`, then check your answer again.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.title: Inspect the Tracked Edit
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

Read the unstaged diff for `volunteer.md` and confirm the old and new meeting times.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: A file diff explains the exact content change behind the status marker.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.title: Stage the Verified Edit
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

Stage `volunteer.md`, then inspect the staged name and staged patch.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: Staging a verified edit prepares exactly that version of the tracked file for the next snapshot.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.solutionCode:
git add volunteer.md
git diff --staged --name-only
git diff --staged -- volunteer.md
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

Stage `volunteer.md`, then inspect the staged name and staged patch.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.title: Commit and Verify the Update
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

The meeting-time edit is staged. Commit it as `Update volunteer meeting time`, then verify the latest history entry and clean status.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: After commit, the updated tracked file becomes part of HEAD and the working tree can be clean again.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Update volunteer meeting time"
git log -1 --oneline
git status --short
try_modify_and_verify_tracked_files_sket.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-notes/` repository.

The meeting-time edit is staged. Commit it as `Update volunteer meeting time`, then verify the latest history entry and clean status.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-notes
mkdir -p release-notes
git -C release-notes init -q -b main
git -C release-notes config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-notes
printf %s 'Meeting: 4 PM
' > volunteer.md
cd ..
git -C release-notes add -A
git -C release-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-notes
printf %s 'Meeting: 5 PM
' > volunteer.md
cd ..
git -C release-notes add -- volunteer.md

' > volunteer.md
cd ..

git -C release-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-notes
printf %s 'Meeting: 5 PM
' > volunteer.md
cd ..
git -C release-notes add -- volunteer.md

single-choice-1.title: Understanding Git Status Output
single-choice-1.prompt: What does the `git status` command show you?
single-choice-1.hint: Think about the information `git status` provides about your files.

help.concept: `git status` provides information about the current state of the working directory and staging area.
help.hint_1: It shows which files are staged, unstaged, or untracked.
help.hint_2: It helps you understand what changes are ready to be committed.

options.c: The state of the working directory and staging area

single-choice-2.title: Purpose of Git Diff
single-choice-2.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git diff` command?
single-choice-2.hint: Consider what `git diff` helps you see about your files.

help.concept: `git diff` shows the differences between the working directory and the last commit.
help.hint_1: It helps you review changes before staging them.
help.hint_2: It shows line-by-line differences in files.

options.d: To show differences between the working directory and the last commit

multi-choice-1.title: Git Status Information
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which of the following does `git status` provide information about? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Consider what `git status` tells you about your files and repository.

help.concept: `git status` provides information about the working directory and staging area.
help.hint_1: It shows which files are staged, unstaged, or untracked.
help.hint_2: It helps you understand what changes are ready to be committed.

drag-reorder-1.title: Git Workflow Steps
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps of a typical Git workflow in the correct order.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Think about the sequence of actions from modifying a file to committing it.

help.concept: A typical Git workflow involves modifying files, staging changes, and committing them.

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Staging Changes
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Complete the command to stage changes in `file.txt`: `git [blank1] file.txt`.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Think about the command used to stage changes.

help.concept: The `git the missing term` command stages changes for the next commit.
help.hint_1: The command moves changes to the staging area.
help.hint_2: It's the command used before committing changes.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Committing Changes
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: Complete the command to commit changes with a message: `git commit -m [blank1]`.
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Think about how to include a message with your commit.

help.concept: The `-m` option allows you to add a commit message directly in the command.
help.hint_1: The message should describe the changes made.

sketch0.title: Begin from a Known State
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
Before editing, confirm the repository is clean with `git status --short`. A quiet result means the working tree matches HEAD.

Make the requested change to a tracked file, then run status again. Git reports a modification because it can compare the current file with its committed version.

Starting from a known state keeps new work separate from leftovers.

sketch1.title: Inspect the Tracked Edit
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Use `git diff` to review the line-level change before staging:

```bash
git diff handbook.md
```

The path narrows the review to one file. Read the removed and added lines and confirm they match the task.

If the diff contains extra work, fix the file before moving forward.

sketch2.title: Stage the Reviewed Version
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
Once the diff is correct, stage the file and inspect the staged snapshot:

```bash
git add handbook.md
git diff --staged handbook.md
```

The second command proves which version is ready to commit. A later edit would appear separately as an unstaged change.

This two-review pattern makes the boundary explicit.

sketch3.title: Commit and Recheck
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
Commit the focused update with a message that describes its purpose, then run status again.

```bash
git commit -m "Clarify volunteer check-in"
git status --short
```

A clean result confirms the tracked edit is now part of history and no accidental work remains.
